docs.title: Basics
docs.text: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is used to style HTML elements — controlling their layout, color, spacing, fonts, and more.

docs.title: 1. CSS Syntax
docs.text:
selector {
  property: value;
}

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
p {
  color: blue;
  font-size: 16px;
}

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
p {
  color: blue;
  font-size: 16px;
}

docs.title: 2. Adding CSS to HTML
docs.text: There are 3 ways to apply CSS:

docs.title: a) Inline CSS
docs.text: <p style="color: red;">Inline styled paragraph</p>

docs.title: b) Internal CSS (within <style> tag)
docs.text:
<head>
  <style>
    h1 {
      text-align: center;
      color: green;
    }
  </style>
</head>

docs.title: c) External CSS (best practice)
docs.text:
Inside <head> in index.html

<link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">

And in styles.css:

body {
  background-color: #f0f0f0;
}

docs.title: 3. Comments in CSS
docs.text: /* This is a CSS comment */

docs.title: 4. Colors in CSS
docs.text:
You can use:

  • Named colors (red, blue, green)
  • Hex codes (#ff0000)
  • RGB (rgb(255, 0, 0))
  • HSL (hsl(0, 100%, 50%))
body {
  background-color: #121212;
  color: rgb(255, 255, 255);
}

docs.title: 5. Inheritance & Specificity
docs.text:
• Some CSS properties (like color, font-family) are inherited by default
  • Specificity decides which rule wins if multiple styles apply
  • Inline > ID > Class > Element selector
<style>
  #heading { color: red; }
  .title { color: blue; }
  h1 { color: green; }
</style>

<h1 id="heading" class="title">Hello</h1>

Output color: Red (#heading wins)

docs.title: Selectors
docs.text: CSS selectors target HTML elements to apply styles. They determine what gets styled.

docs.title: a) Element Selector
docs.text:
Targets all elements of a specific type.

p {
  color: black;
}

docs.title: b) Class Selector
docs.text:
Targets elements with a specific class.

.title {
  font-weight: bold;
}

docs.title: c) ID Selector
docs.text:
Targets a unique element with a specific id.

#main-heading {
  font-size: 32px;
}

docs.title: 2. Grouping Selectors
docs.text:
Apply the same style to multiple selectors.

h1, h2, h3 {
  font-family: Arial;
}

docs.title: a) Descendant Selector
docs.text:
Targets elements nested inside another.

div p {
  color: gray;
}

docs.title: b) Child Selector (>)
docs.text:
Targets direct children only.

ul > li {
  list-style: none;
}

docs.title: c) Adjacent Sibling (+)
docs.text:
Targets the first element immediately after another.

h1 + p {
  margin-top: 0;
}

docs.title: d) General Sibling (~)
docs.text:
Targets all siblings after the first.

h1 ~ p {
  color: blue;
}

docs.title: 4. Attribute Selectors
docs.text:
Target elements based on attributes.

input[type="text"] {
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
}

docs.title: 5. Pseudo-classes
docs.text:
Target elements based on state or position.

a:hover {
  text-decoration: underline;
}

li:first-child {
  font-weight: bold;
}

docs.title: 6. Pseudo-elements
docs.text:
Style parts of elements.

p::first-line {
  font-variant: small-caps;
}

docs.title: Fonts
docs.text: Fonts control the appearance of text — including typeface, size, weight, spacing, and more.

docs.title: 1. Font Family
docs.text:
Set the typeface using font-family. Always include a fallback.

body {
  font-family: 'Arial', 'Helvetica', sans-serif;
}

docs.title: 2. Font Size
docs.text:
Controls the size of the text.

p {
  font-size: 16px;
}

docs.title: Common units:
docs.text:
• px — absolute (pixels)
  • em — relative to parent
  • rem — relative to root (html)
  • % — relative to parent
h1 {
  font-size: 2em;  /* 2x the parent size */
}

docs.title: 3. Font Weight
docs.text:
Controls thickness of text.

strong {
  font-weight: bold; /* or numeric: 100 to 900 */
}

docs.title: 4. Font Style
docs.text:
Used for italics and oblique styles.

em {
  font-style: italic;
}

docs.title: 5. Font Variant
docs.text:
Used for small-caps.

p {
  font-variant: small-caps;
}

docs.title: 6. Line Height
docs.text:
Controls vertical spacing between lines of text.

p {
  line-height: 1.6;
}

docs.title: 7. Shorthand Property
docs.text:
Combine multiple font properties.

p {
  font: italic small-caps bold 16px/1.5 'Georgia', serif;
}

docs.title: 8. Web Fonts
docs.text: Import custom fonts using @font-face or via Google Fonts:

docs.title: Example (Google Fonts):
docs.text:
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Roboto&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">

body {
  font-family: 'Roboto', sans-serif;
}

docs.title: Text
docs.text: CSS provides properties to style and align text content in HTML elements.

docs.title: 1. Text Color
docs.text:
p {
  color: #333;
}

docs.title: 2. Text Alignment
docs.text:
h1 {
  text-align: center;
}

Values:

  • left (default)
  • right
  • center
  • justify

docs.title: 3. Text Decoration
docs.text:
Used for underlines, overlines, strikethroughs.

a {
  text-decoration: none;
}

Common values:

  • none
  • underline
  • overline
  • line-through

docs.title: 4. Text Transform
docs.text:
Controls capitalization.

h2 {
  text-transform: uppercase;
}

Options:

  • none
  • capitalize
  • uppercase
  • lowercase

docs.title: 5. Letter Spacing
docs.text:
Space between letters.

p {
  letter-spacing: 1px;
}

docs.title: 6. Word Spacing
docs.text:
Space between words.

p {
  word-spacing: 5px;
}

docs.title: 7. Line Height
docs.text:
Controls spacing between lines.

p {
  line-height: 1.5;
}

docs.title: 8. Text Shadow
docs.text:
Adds shadow to text.

h1 {
  text-shadow: 2px 2px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);
}

Syntax: x-offset y-offset blur-radius color

docs.title: Colors
docs.text: CSS allows you to style elements with different colors for text, backgrounds, borders, and more.

docs.title: Text Color
docs.text:
p {
  color: red;
}

docs.title: Background Color
docs.text:
div {
  background-color: #f0f0f0;
}

docs.title: Border Color
docs.text:
input {
  border: 1px solid #333;
}

docs.title: a) Named Colors
docs.text:
Predefined color names.

h1 {
  color: blue;
}

docs.title: b) Hexadecimal
docs.text:
p {
  color: #ff0000; /* red */
}

docs.title: c) RGB
docs.text:
div {
  background-color: rgb(255, 255, 255);
}

docs.title: d) RGBA (with transparency)
docs.text:
div {
  background-color: rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5);
}

docs.title: e) HSL
docs.text:
Hue, Saturation, Lightness.

h2 {
  color: hsl(240, 100%, 50%);
}

docs.title: f) HSLA (with alpha)
docs.text:
section {
  background-color: hsla(120, 100%, 50%, 0.3);
}

docs.title: 3. Transparent Keyword
docs.text:
div {
  background-color: transparent;
}

docs.title: Specificity & Cascade
docs.text: When multiple CSS rules target the same element, the cascade and specificity determine which rule wins.

docs.title: 1. The Cascade
docs.text:
CSS applies rules based on:

  1. Source order (last rule wins if equal specificity)
  2. Importance (!important)
  3. Specificity
  4. Origin (browser default < user styles < author styles)

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
p {
  color: blue;
}

p {
  color: red;
}

Result: Red (red overrides blue because it comes later)

docs.title: 2. Specificity
docs.text:
Each selector has a specificity value:

Selector Type Specificity Value Inline styles 1000 ID selectors 100 Class/attr/pseudo-class 10 Element/pseudo-element 1

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
/* Specificity: 1 */
p {
  color: green;
}

/* Specificity: 10 */
.text {
  color: blue;
}

/* Specificity: 100 */
#main {
  color: red;
}

Result: Red wins due to higher specificity.

docs.title: 3. !important
docs.text:
Overrides all specificity rules (except other !important rules with higher specificity).

p {
  color: blue !important;
}

Use sparingly – it's hard to override and often a sign of poor structure.

docs.title: 4. Specificity Comparison
docs.text: Selector Specificity h1 1 .title 10 #header 100 style="..." 1000 !important ∞ (kind of)

docs.title: Sizing Units
docs.text: CSS sizing units define dimensions like width, height, padding, margin, and font-size. They fall into two main categories: absolute and relative units.

docs.title: 1. Absolute Units
docs.text:
These do not scale with screen size or context — they remain fixed.

Unit Name Description px Pixels Most commonly used; screen-relative pt Points 1pt = 1/72 inch (used in print) cm Centimeters Physical measurement mm Millimeters Physical measurement in Inches 1in = 96px (standard)

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
.box {
  width: 200px;
  font-size: 12pt;
}

Use when: you want precise control (e.g. fixed UI components), but avoid for responsive designs.

docs.title: 2. Relative Units
docs.text: These scale based on parent elements, root, or viewport.

docs.title: a) % — Percentage
docs.text:
Relative to the parent element's size.

div {
  width: 50%; /* 50% of parent */
}

docs.title: b) em — Relative to Parent Font Size
docs.text:
1 em = font-size of the current element’s parent.

/* If parent has 16px font size */
p {
  font-size: 2em; /* 32px */
}

Stacking ems can cause unintended scaling (compound effect).

docs.title: c) rem — Relative to Root Element
docs.text:
1 rem = font-size of the html element.

html {
  font-size: 16px;
}

h1 {
  font-size: 2rem; /* 32px */
}

Preferred over em for consistent, predictable sizing.

docs.title: d) vw, vh, vmin, vmax — Viewport Units
3. calc() — Dynamic Calculations
docs.text:
Combines units for flexible sizing.

.container {
  width: calc(100% - 60px);
  height: calc(50vh + 100px);
}

Useful for subtracting fixed elements like headers or sidebars.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use rem for font sizes (scales with user settings).
  • Use px or % for borders, paddings, depending on layout needs.
  • Use vw, vh for responsive layouts.
  • Avoid absolute units like cm, pt in screen designs.

docs.title: Display Property
docs.text: The display property defines how an element is rendered in the document flow — block, inline, grid, flex, etc.

docs.title: a) block
docs.text:
• Takes up the full width
  • Starts on a new line
div {
  display: block;
}

Examples: <div>, <p>, <h1> (default block elements)

docs.title: b) inline
docs.text:
• Takes up only as much width as needed
  • Flows inline with text
  • Cannot set width/height
span {
  display: inline;
}

Examples: <span>, <a>, <strong> (default inline elements)

docs.title: c) inline-block
docs.text:
• Behaves like inline (flows with text)
  • Allows width/height to be set
button {
  display: inline-block;
  width: 100px;
  height: 40px;
}

docs.title: d) none
docs.text:
• Completely hides the element
  • Removes it from layout and DOM flow
p {
  display: none;
}

docs.title: a) flex
docs.text:
Enables flexbox layout for flexible, one-dimensional layouts.

.container {
  display: flex;
}

Use with properties like justify-content, align-items, etc.

docs.title: b) grid
docs.text:
Enables CSS grid layout — two-dimensional control.

.container {
  display: grid;
}

Use with grid-template-columns, grid-gap, etc.

docs.title: c) inline-flex / inline-grid
docs.text:
Same as flex/grid, but behaves like inline.

span {
  display: inline-flex;
}

docs.title: table, table-row, table-cell, etc.
docs.text:
Mimic HTML table behaviors.

div {
  display: table;
}

docs.title: 4. Default Values by Tag
docs.text: Tag Default display <div> block <span> inline <img> inline-block <table> table

docs.title: 5. Visual Summary
docs.text: Display New Line Width/Height Allowed Layout Control block ✅ ✅ ❌ inline ❌ ❌ ❌ inline-block ❌ ✅ ❌ flex ✅ ✅ ✅ (1D) grid ✅ ✅ ✅ (2D)

docs.title: Overflow
docs.text: The overflow property controls what happens when content exceeds the size of its container.

docs.title: a) visible (default)
docs.text:
Content spills out of the box — no clipping.

.box {
  overflow: visible;
}

docs.title: b) hidden
docs.text:
Extra content is cut off and not visible.

.box {
  overflow: hidden;
}

Used when you want a clean container without scroll.

docs.title: c) scroll
docs.text:
Adds scrollbars (always visible), regardless of need.

.box {
  overflow: scroll;
}

Bad UX if scrolling isn’t needed — use auto instead.

docs.title: d) auto
docs.text:
Adds scrollbars only if needed.

.box {
  overflow: auto;
}

Best for responsive and accessible designs.

docs.title: 2. Overflow Axes
docs.text:
You can control each axis individually:

.box {
  overflow-x: auto;
  overflow-y: hidden;
}

docs.title: Example 1: Truncate Text
docs.text:
.truncate {
  white-space: nowrap;
  overflow: hidden;
  text-overflow: ellipsis;
}

docs.title: Example 2: Scrollable Container
docs.text:
.scroll-area {
  height: 200px;
  overflow-y: auto;
}

docs.title: 4. Notes
docs.text:
• Overflow only works on block-level or positioned elements with a defined size (height or width).
  • For inline elements, overflow has no effect unless converted with display: block or similar.

docs.title: Position
docs.text: The position property defines how an element is placed in the document — static, relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky.

docs.title: 1. static (default)
docs.text:
• Normal document flow
  • top, left, right, bottom have no effect
div {
  position: static;
}

docs.title: 2. relative
docs.text:
• Positioned relative to its normal position
  • Leaves original space in the flow
.box {
  position: relative;
  top: 10px;
  left: 20px;
}

Used to nudge elements or create a reference for absolutely positioned children.

docs.title: 3. absolute
docs.text:
• Removed from document flow
  • Positioned relative to the nearest positioned ancestor
  • If none, it uses the html (viewport)
.child {
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;
  right: 0;
}

Requires parent to be relative, absolute, or fixed if you want local positioning.

docs.title: 4. fixed
docs.text:
• Removed from flow
  • Positioned relative to the viewport
  • Stays in place when scrolling
.header {
  position: fixed;
  top: 0;
  width: 100%;
}

Used for navbars, back-to-top buttons, etc.

docs.title: 5. sticky
docs.text:
• Acts like relative until a threshold is reached, then becomes fixed
  • Requires a top/left/right/bottom value
.menu {
  position: sticky;
  top: 0;
}

Parent must have height for sticky to work properly.

docs.title: 6. Z-Index
docs.text:
Use z-index to control stacking order (higher = on top).

.box {
  position: absolute;
  z-index: 10;
}

Only works on positioned elements (not static).

docs.title: Summary Table
docs.text: Position In Flow Offset Allowed Scrolls with Page Reference Point static ✅ ❌ ✅ Normal document flow relative ✅ ✅ ✅ Own original position absolute ❌ ✅ ❌ Nearest positioned ancestor fixed ❌ ✅ ❌ Viewport sticky ✅/❌ ✅ ✅/❌ Toggles: self → viewport

docs.title: Variables
docs.text: CSS Variables (also called custom properties) allow you to store values in reusable, scoped variables using the -- syntax.

docs.title: 1. Declaring a Variable
docs.text:
Defined inside a selector (commonly :root for global scope):

:root {
  --main-color: #3498db;
  --padding-base: 1rem;
}

docs.title: 2. Using a Variable
docs.text:
Use the var() function:

button {
  background-color: var(--main-color);
  padding: var(--padding-base);
}

docs.title: 3. Fallback Value
docs.text:
Use a default if the variable is undefined:

p {
  color: var(--text-color, black);
}

docs.title: 4. Scope
docs.text:
Variables are scoped to the selector they are declared in:

.container {
  --local-color: red;
}

.child {
  color: var(--local-color); /* works only if .child is inside .container */
}

docs.title: 5. Dynamic Theming
docs.text:
CSS variables can be updated dynamically (e.g. in dark mode):

:root {
  --bg-color: white;
  --text-color: black;
}

.dark-mode {
  --bg-color: #121212;
  --text-color: #f0f0f0;
}

body {
  background: var(--bg-color);
  color: var(--text-color);
}

docs.title: 6. Inside Media Queries
docs.text:
Variables can be redefined based on screen size:

:root {
  --font-size: 16px;
}

@media (min-width: 768px) {
  :root {
    --font-size: 18px;
  }
}

body {
  font-size: var(--font-size);
}

docs.title: 7. Invalid Use Cases
docs.text:
CSS variables do not work in:

  • Media query expressions
  • Selectors
  • Keyframe names
  • @import paths
/* ❌ INVALID */
@media (min-width: var(--breakpoint)) { ... }

docs.title: Media Queries
docs.text: Media queries let you apply CSS based on screen/device properties, enabling responsive design.

docs.title: 1. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
@media media-type and (condition) {
  /* CSS rules */
}

Example:

@media screen and (max-width: 768px) {
  body {
    font-size: 14px;
  }
}

docs.title: 2. Media Types
docs.text: Type Description all Default, applies to all devices screen Computer screens, tablets, etc. print Print previews and printers speech Screen readers

docs.title: 3. Common Features (Conditions)
docs.text: Feature Example Description max-width max-width: 600px Target screens ≤ 600px min-width min-width: 1024px Target screens ≥ 1024px max-height max-height: 500px Target short screens orientation orientation: portrait Portrait vs. landscape prefers-color-scheme dark/light Light/dark mode detection

docs.title: 4. Responsive Breakpoints (Example)
docs.text:
/* Mobile first */
body {
  font-size: 14px;
}

@media (min-width: 600px) {
  body {
    font-size: 16px;
  }
}

@media (min-width: 1024px) {
  body {
    font-size: 18px;
  }
}

docs.title: 5. Multiple Conditions
docs.text:
@media (min-width: 600px) and (max-width: 1024px) {
  .box {
    background: yellow;
  }
}

Use , for OR conditions:

@media (max-width: 500px), (min-width: 1200px) {
  .header {
    display: none;
  }
}

docs.title: 6. Dark Mode Example
docs.text:
@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
  body {
    background: #121212;
    color: #f0f0f0;
  }
}

docs.title: 7. Print Styles
docs.text:
@media print {
  nav, footer {
    display: none;
  }

  body {
    font-size: 12pt;
  }
}

docs.title: Float and Clear
docs.text: The float property allows elements to be taken out of normal flow and positioned to the left or right, with text/content flowing around them. clear is used to prevent wrapping around floated elements.

docs.title: 1. float Property
docs.text: Value Description left Floats the element to the left right Floats the element to the right none Default, no float

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
img {
  float: left;
  margin-right: 10px;
}

Result: The image floats to the left, and the text wraps around it on the right.

docs.title: 2. Effects of Float
docs.text:
• Floated elements are removed from normal flow
  • Parent containers may collapse if all children are floated

docs.title: 3. clear Property
docs.text:
Prevents elements from wrapping around floated siblings.

Value Description left Clears floats on the left side right Clears floats on the right side both Clears both sides none Default

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
.clearfix {
  clear: both;
}

Used to push content below floated elements.

docs.title: 4. Clearing Floats (Fixing Collapsing Parent)
docs.text: Floats can cause a container to collapse if it has no non-floated children.

docs.title: a) Using Clearfix
docs.text:
.clearfix::after {
  content: "";
  display: table;
  clear: both;
}

Apply .clearfix to the parent container.

docs.title: 5. Float vs Flex/Grid
docs.text:
Use float only for small layouts (e.g., image wrap).

Avoid it for full layouts — prefer flex or grid for proper alignment, responsiveness, and readability.

docs.title: Flexbox
docs.text: Flexbox (Flexible Box Layout) is a 1-dimensional layout system that makes it easy to align, distribute, and order space among items in a container — either horizontally or vertically.

docs.title: 1. Enabling Flex
docs.text:
.container {
  display: flex;
}

This makes all direct children flex items.

docs.title: 2. Main vs Cross Axis
docs.text:
• Main Axis: Defined by flex-direction
  • Cross Axis: Perpendicular to the main axis
flex-direction value Main Axis Cross Axis row (default) horizontal vertical column vertical horizontal

docs.title: 3. flex-direction
docs.text:
.container {
  flex-direction: row | row-reverse | column | column-reverse;
}

docs.title: 4. justify-content (Main Axis)
docs.text:
Aligns items along the main axis.

.container {
  justify-content: flex-start | flex-end | center | space-between | space-around | space-evenly;
}

docs.title: 5. align-items (Cross Axis)
docs.text:
Aligns items along the cross axis.

.container {
  align-items: flex-start | flex-end | center | stretch | baseline;
}

docs.title: 6. align-content (Cross Axis, multiple rows)
docs.text:
Used when there are multiple lines (wrapping).

.container {
  align-content: flex-start | center | space-between;
}

docs.title: 7. flex-wrap
docs.text:
Controls whether items wrap to the next line.

.container {
  flex-wrap: nowrap | wrap | wrap-reverse;
}

docs.title: 8. Shorthand: flex-flow
docs.text:
Combines flex-direction + flex-wrap

.container {
  flex-flow: row wrap;
}

docs.title: a) order
docs.text:
Controls item visual order.

.item {
  order: 2; /* Default is 0 */
}

docs.title: b) flex-grow
docs.text:
Defines how much the item expands relative to others.

.item {
  flex-grow: 1;
}

docs.title: c) flex-shrink
docs.text:
Defines how much the item shrinks.

.item {
  flex-shrink: 0;
}

docs.title: d) flex-basis
docs.text:
Sets the initial size before growing/shrinking.

.item {
  flex-basis: 200px;
}

docs.title: e) Shorthand: flex
docs.text:
.item {
  flex: 1 1 200px; /* grow shrink basis */
}

docs.title: 10. align-self
docs.text:
Overrides align-items for a single item.

.item {
  align-self: center;
}

docs.title: Example Layout
docs.text:
.container {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  align-items: center;
}

<div class="container">
  <div>Item 1</div>
  <div>Item 2</div>
  <div>Item 3</div>
</div>

docs.title: Grid
docs.text: Grid is a 2-dimensional layout system for arranging items in rows and columns.

docs.title: 1. Enabling Grid
docs.text:
.container {
  display: grid;
}

All direct children become grid items.

docs.title: 2. Defining Columns and Rows
docs.text:
.container {
  grid-template-columns: 200px 1fr 2fr;
  grid-template-rows: 100px auto;
}
  • px = fixed size
  • fr = fraction of remaining space
  • auto = size based on content

docs.title: 3. Repeat Notation
docs.text:
grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);

Same as: 1fr 1fr 1fr

docs.title: 4. Gap Between Items
docs.text:
.container {
  gap: 20px; /* row + column gap */
}

Split version:

row-gap: 10px;
column-gap: 15px;

docs.title: 5. Placing Items
docs.text:
.item {
  grid-column: 1 / 3; /* spans columns 1 and 2 */
  grid-row: 2 / 4;
}

Or shorthand:

grid-area: row-start / column-start / row-end / column-end;

docs.title: 6. Named Areas
docs.text:
.container {
  grid-template-areas:
    "header header"
    "sidebar main"
    "footer footer";
}

.header {
  grid-area: header;
}

docs.title: 7. Implicit Tracks
docs.text:
Grid auto-generates rows/columns if needed.

.container {
  grid-auto-rows: 100px;
}

docs.title: 8. Auto-Placement
docs.text:
Items are placed in the next available cell by default.

.container {
  grid-auto-flow: row | column | dense;
}

docs.title: 9. Responsive Grid
docs.text:
.container {
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr));
}

This makes columns responsive and wrap when space is tight.

docs.title: 10. Aligning Content
docs.text:
• Align grid items:
justify-items: start | center | end | stretch;
align-items: start | center | end | stretch;
  • Align the grid as a whole:
justify-content: center | space-between | space-around;
align-content: center;

docs.title: Transforms
docs.text: The transform property lets you apply 2D and 3D transformations to elements — such as rotating, scaling, moving, or skewing.

docs.title: 1. translate(x, y)
docs.text:
Moves an element from its current position.

.box {
  transform: translate(50px, 20px);
}
  • x = horizontal shift
  • y = vertical shift

docs.title: 2. scale(x, y)
docs.text:
Resizes the element.

.box {
  transform: scale(1.5); /* 1.5x both axes */
}

transform: scale(1.2, 0.8); /* x = 120%, y = 80% */

docs.title: 3. rotate(angle)
docs.text:
Rotates the element around its center.

.box {
  transform: rotate(45deg);
}

Use negative angles for counter-clockwise rotation.

docs.title: 4. skew(x, y)
docs.text:
Skews (slants) the element.

.box {
  transform: skew(20deg, 10deg);
}

docs.title: 5. Combine Multiple Transforms
docs.text:
.box {
  transform: translateX(50px) rotate(15deg) scale(1.2);
}

They are applied in order, left to right.

docs.title: 6. transform-origin
docs.text:
Sets the point of transformation.

.box {
  transform: rotate(45deg);
  transform-origin: top left;
}

Default is center center.

docs.title: 7. 3D Transforms (Basic)
docs.text:
Enable 3D perspective with:

.container {
  perspective: 1000px;
}

.box {
  transform: rotateY(45deg);
}

docs.title: 8. Performance Note
docs.text: Transforms are GPU-accelerated and more performant than changing top, left, etc. Use them for animations/movement.

docs.title: Transitions
docs.text: CSS transitions let you smoothly animate changes in CSS property values over time.

docs.title: 1. Basic Syntax
docs.text:
.element {
  transition: property duration timing-function delay;
}

Example:

.button {
  background-color: blue;
  transition: background-color 0.3s ease-in-out;
}

.button:hover {
  background-color: red;
}

When :hover is triggered, the background smoothly changes from blue to red.

docs.title: 2. Shorthand Breakdown
docs.text:
transition: all 0.5s ease 0.1s;
  • property — what to animate (color, all, etc.)
  • duration — how long the transition lasts (s or ms)
  • timing-function — how the animation accelerates
  • delay — time before transition starts

docs.title: 3. Timing Functions
docs.text: Value Description linear Constant speed ease Slow → fast → slow ease-in Slow start ease-out Slow end ease-in-out Slow start and end cubic-bezier(...) Custom easing curve

docs.title: 4. Multiple Properties
docs.text:
.box {
  transition: width 0.3s ease, background-color 0.3s linear;
}

Each property can have its own config.

docs.title: 5. Transitionable Properties
docs.text:
Only animatable properties can be transitioned:

✅ Examples:

  • color
  • background-color
  • width, height
  • transform
  • opacity
  • margin, padding

❌ Not animatable:

  • display
  • position
  • visibility (though can be paired with opacity)

docs.title: 6. Tips
docs.text:
• Use transition: all with caution — it's less performant and harder to debug.
  • Transitions only work when there's a change in property value (e.g. via :hover, .active, JS toggle).

docs.title: 7. Example: Button Hover
docs.text:
.button {
  background-color: #333;
  color: white;
  transition: background-color 0.3s ease, transform 0.2s;
}

.button:hover {
  background-color: #555;
  transform: scale(1.05);
}

docs.title: Animations
docs.text: CSS animations let you create complex, keyframe-based transitions between styles over time — without JavaScript.

docs.title: 1. Basic Structure
docs.text:
.element {
  animation: name duration timing-function delay iteration-count direction fill-mode;
}

Only animation-name and animation-duration are required.

docs.title: 2. Example
docs.text:
@keyframes slideIn {
  from {
    transform: translateX(-100%);
  }
  to {
    transform: translateX(0);
  }
}

.box {
  animation: slideIn 0.5s ease-out;
}

docs.title: a) animation-name
docs.text:
The name of the @keyframes.

animation-name: fadeIn;

docs.title: b) animation-duration
docs.text:
How long the animation takes.

animation-duration: 1s;

docs.title: c) animation-timing-function
docs.text:
Controls animation pacing.

animation-timing-function: ease | linear | ease-in | ease-out | ease-in-out | cubic-bezier();

docs.title: d) animation-delay
docs.text:
Wait time before animation starts.

animation-delay: 0.5s;

docs.title: e) animation-iteration-count
docs.text:
Number of repeats.

animation-iteration-count: 3;   /* 3 times */
animation-iteration-count: infinite;

docs.title: f) animation-direction
docs.text:
Direction of each cycle.

normal | reverse | alternate | alternate-reverse;

docs.title: g) animation-fill-mode
docs.text:
Defines how styles apply before/after animation.

none | forwards | backwards | both;

Example:

animation-fill-mode: forwards;

Keeps final keyframe styles after animation ends.

docs.title: 4. Shorthand
docs.text: animation: fadeIn 1s ease-in 0.2s 1 normal forwards;

docs.title: 5. Pausing/Controlling
docs.text:
.element {
  animation-play-state: paused | running;
}

docs.title: 6. Multiple Animations
docs.text: animation: move 1s ease, fade 2s linear;

docs.title: 7. Example: Bounce
docs.text:
@keyframes bounce {
  0%, 100% {
    transform: translateY(0);
  }
  50% {
    transform: translateY(-20px);
  }
}

.ball {
  animation: bounce 0.6s infinite;
}

docs.title: object-fit and object-position
docs.text: The object-fit property defines how content like images or videos are resized to fit their container.

docs.title: Syntax:
docs.text:
img {
  object-fit: value;
}

docs.title: Values:
docs.text: Value Description fill Default. Stretches to fill container — may distort aspect ratio. contain Fits inside container without cropping — maintains aspect ratio. cover Fills entire container — may crop, maintains aspect ratio. none Image keeps original size — no resize. scale-down Chooses the smaller result of none or contain.

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
img {
  width: 300px;
  height: 200px;
  object-fit: cover;
}

Result: The image fills the 300×200 box, cropped if necessary.

docs.title: 2. object-position
docs.text: Controls alignment of the image inside its box when object-fit ≠ fill.

docs.title: Syntax:
docs.text:
img {
  object-position: center top;
}

docs.title: Common values:
docs.text:
• center, top, bottom, left, right
  • You can also use px, %

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
img {
  object-fit: cover;
  object-position: top;
}

Result: Image fills the container, cropping from the bottom if needed.

docs.title: 3. Practical Use Case: Responsive Image Crop
docs.text:
img {
  width: 100%;
  height: 300px;
  object-fit: cover;
  object-position: center;
}
  • Maintains aspect ratio
  • Crops the excess
  • Ensures consistent layout

docs.title: Filters
docs.text: The filter property applies visual effects (like blur, brightness, contrast, etc.) to elements — especially images and backgrounds.

docs.title: 1. Syntax
docs.text:
.element {
  filter: filter-function(value);
}

Multiple filters can be chained:

filter: brightness(1.2) contrast(90%) saturate(150%);

docs.title: a) blur(px)
docs.text:
Applies Gaussian blur.

filter: blur(5px);

docs.title: b) brightness(%)
docs.text:
Controls light intensity.

filter: brightness(150%); /* brighter */
filter: brightness(50%);  /* darker */

docs.title: c) contrast(%)
docs.text:
Adjusts contrast.

filter: contrast(200%);

docs.title: d) grayscale(%)
docs.text:
Converts to grayscale.

filter: grayscale(100%);

docs.title: e) sepia(%)
docs.text:
Applies a warm, brown tone.

filter: sepia(100%);

docs.title: f) saturate(%)
docs.text:
Controls color intensity.

filter: saturate(200%);

docs.title: g) hue-rotate(deg)
docs.text:
Shifts hue (color spectrum rotation).

filter: hue-rotate(90deg);

docs.title: h) invert(%)
docs.text:
Inverts colors.

filter: invert(100%);

docs.title: i) opacity(%)
docs.text:
Sets element transparency.

filter: opacity(70%);

docs.title: 3. Example: Hover Filter
docs.text:
img {
  filter: grayscale(100%);
  transition: filter 0.3s;
}

img:hover {
  filter: grayscale(0%);
}

docs.title: 4. Notes
docs.text:
• Filters affect rendering — may impact performance.
  • Works on most elements (<img>, <div>, pseudo-elements, etc.).
  • Combine with transitions for smooth effects.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: Express.js is a minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework that provides robust features for building web and mobile applications. It simplifies the process of handling routes, requests, responses, and middleware in Node.js.

docs.title: 1. Why Use Express.js?
docs.text:
• Simplifies building server-side applications with Node.js
  • Provides routing, middleware, and templating support
  • Lightweight and fast
  • Widely used in building REST APIs and full-stack apps
  • Easy integration with databases like MongoDB, MySQL, PostgreSQL

docs.title: 2. Installation
docs.text:
First, initialize a Node.js project:

mkdir express-app
cd express-app
npm init -y
````

Install Express:

```bash
npm install express

docs.title: 3. Basic Express Server
docs.text:
const express = require("express");
const app = express();
const PORT = 3000;

app.get("/", (req, res) => {
  res.send("Hello, Express!");
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running on http://localhost:${PORT}`);
});
  • express() creates an Express app
  • app.get(path, callback) handles GET requests
  • res.send() sends a response
  • app.listen() starts the server

docs.title: 4. Handling Different Routes
docs.text:
app.get("/about", (req, res) => {
  res.send("About Page");
});

app.post("/submit", (req, res) => {
  res.send("Form Submitted");
});

docs.title: 5. Route Parameters (req.params)
docs.text:
Route parameters allow you to capture values from the URL.

app.get("/users/:id", (req, res) => {
  const userId = req.params.id;
  res.send(`User ID: ${userId}`);
});
  • Visiting /users/42 → req.params.id is "42"
  • Useful for dynamic routes like user profiles, product pages, etc.

Multiple parameters:

app.get("/posts/:postId/comments/:commentId", (req, res) => {
  const { postId, commentId } = req.params;
  res.send(`Post ID: ${postId}, Comment ID: ${commentId}`);
});

docs.title: 6. Middleware
docs.text:
Middleware functions are functions that run before reaching the final route handler. They can modify req and res.

app.use((req, res, next) => {
  console.log(`${req.method} ${req.url}`);
  next();
});

docs.title: 7. Serving Static Files
docs.text:
app.use(express.static("public"));

Now you can serve files like public/index.html.

docs.title: 8. JSON Data Handling
docs.text:
app.use(express.json());

app.post("/data", (req, res) => {
  res.json({ received: req.body });
});
  • express.json() parses JSON request bodies

docs.title: 9. Express Routing in Separate Files
docs.text:
// routes/user.js
const express = require("express");
const router = express.Router();

router.get("/", (req, res) => res.send("User List"));
router.get("/:id", (req, res) => res.send(`User ${req.params.id}`));

module.exports = router;

// app.js
const userRoutes = require("./routes/user");
app.use("/users", userRoutes);

Visiting /users/ → "User List" Visiting /users/42 → "User 42"

docs.title: 1. What is Git?
docs.text:
Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes in source code and helps teams collaborate on projects.

  • Created by Linus Torvalds
  • CLI-based, fast, and efficient
  • Works offline

docs.title: 2. What is GitHub?
docs.text: GitHub is a cloud-based hosting service for Git repositories. It adds collaboration features like pull requests, issues, and CI/CD.

docs.title: 3. Install and Configure Git
docs.text:
Install Git from: git website

Initial setup:

git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
````

Check config:

```bash
git config --list

docs.title: 4. Basic Commands
docs.text:
git init            # Initialize a new repo
git status          # Show file status
git add <file>      # Stage file
git commit -m "msg" # Commit staged changes
git log             # View commit history

Stage all files:

git add .

docs.title: Create a Repository
docs.text:
1. Create a new repo on GitHub (don't initialize with README)
  2. In terminal:
git remote add origin https://github.com/user/repo.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main

docs.title: 6. .gitignore
docs.text:
Use .gitignore to exclude files/folders from being tracked.

Example:

node_modules/
.env
.DS_Store

docs.title: 7. Cloning Repositories
docs.text: git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

docs.title: 8. Branching
docs.text:
git branch         # List branches
git branch dev     # Create branch
git switch dev     # Switch branch (or: git checkout dev)
git merge dev      # Merge dev into current branch

Delete a branch:

git branch -d dev

docs.title: 9. Handling Merge Conflicts
docs.text:
When two branches modify the same line:

<<<<<<< HEAD
your version
=======
incoming version
>>>>>>> dev

Resolve manually, then:

git add .
git commit

docs.title: 10. Undoing Changes
docs.text:
Discard unstaged changes:

git restore <file>

Unstage a file:

git reset <file>

Reset entire repo:

git reset --hard HEAD

Revert a commit (safe):

git revert <commit-hash>

docs.title: 11. Stashing Changes
docs.text:
Temporarily save work:

git stash
git stash list
git stash apply
git stash pop

docs.title: 12. Working with Remotes
docs.text:
git remote -v                # Show remotes
git remote add origin <url>  # Add remote
git push origin main         # Push to remote
git pull origin main         # Pull latest

docs.title: 13. Pull Requests (PRs)
docs.text:
1. Push branch to GitHub
  2. Click "Compare & Pull Request"
  3. Add title, description, reviewers
  4. Merge after approval

docs.title: 14. Fork vs Clone
docs.text:
• Fork: Your own copy of someone else’s repo (for contributing)

  • Clone: Local copy of any repo (for development)

docs.title: 15. Tags and Releases
docs.text:
git tag v1.0
git tag -a v1.1 -m "Version 1.1"
git push origin --tags

Use tags on GitHub to create releases.

docs.title: 16. Rebase (Advanced)
docs.text:
git rebase main         # Rebase current branch onto main
git rebase -i HEAD~3    # Interactive rebase last 3 commits

Squash commits by choosing s for all except the first.

docs.title: HTTPS with Token:
docs.text: git remote set-url origin https://<token>@github.com/user/repo.git

docs.title: SSH:
docs.text:
1. Generate key:
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@example.com"

  1. Add public key to GitHub → Settings → SSH & GPG

  2. Test:

ssh -T git@github.com

docs.title: 18. Git Tools
docs.text:
• GitHub Desktop – GUI for GitHub workflows
  • Sourcetree, GitKraken – Git GUI clients
  • GitLens (VS Code) – Git insights in editor

docs.title: 19. Git Hooks (Optional)
docs.text:
Automate actions on Git events:

Example: .git/hooks/pre-commit

#!/bin/sh
npm run lint

Make it executable:

chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit

docs.title: 20. Best Practices
docs.text:
• Commit often with meaningful messages
  • Use .gitignore to avoid committing unwanted files
  • Use branches for features/fixes
  • Don’t commit secrets (use .env)
  • Resolve merge conflicts carefully
  • Keep pull requests small and focused
  • Never force push to main unless you know what you’re doing

docs.title: Commit Message Format
docs.text:
Use present tense, short, descriptive messages:

git commit -m "Add contact form validation"

Prefix (optional): feat, fix, docs, refactor, test, etc.

docs.title: Summary Cheatsheet
docs.text: Command Description git init Start new repository git clone <url> Clone a repo git status Show status git add . Stage all changes git commit -m "" Commit changes git push origin main Push to GitHub git pull origin main Pull latest from GitHub git branch <name> Create new branch git switch <branch> Switch branch git merge <branch> Merge into current git log Show commit history git stash Save uncommitted work git tag v1.0 Create version tag

docs.title: What is HTML?
docs.text: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language. It is the standard markup language used to create and structure content on the web.

docs.title: Key Points:
docs.text:
• HTML describes the structure of a web page using elements (also called tags).
  • Elements are enclosed in angle brackets: <tagname>...</tagname>.
  • Most elements have an opening tag, content, and a closing tag.
  • HTML files use the .html extension.

docs.title: Basic Boilerplate:
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" />
    <title>Page Title</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <h1>Hello World!</h1>
  </body>
</html>

docs.title: Explanation of Each Part:
docs.text: Section Purpose <!DOCTYPE html> Declares the document type and version (HTML5). Must be the first line. <html lang="en"> Root element of the page. lang="en" indicates English content. <head> Contains metadata about the document (not visible content). <meta charset="UTF-8"> Sets the character encoding (UTF-8 supports all characters). <meta name="viewport"...> Ensures the site is mobile responsive by controlling page scaling. <title> Sets the title shown in the browser tab. <body> Contains everything visible on the page (text, images, elements, etc).

docs.title: Headings
docs.text:
<h1>Heading 1</h1>
<h2>Heading 2</h2>
<!-- up to <h6> -->

docs.title: Paragraphs
docs.text: <p>This is a paragraph.</p>

docs.title: Links
docs.text: <a href="https://example.com">Visit Example</a>

docs.title: Images
docs.text: <img src="image.jpg" alt="Description of image" />

docs.title: Unordered:
docs.text:
<ul>
  <li>Item 1</li>
  <li>Item 2</li>
</ul>

docs.title: Ordered:
docs.text:
<ol>
  <li>First</li>
  <li>Second</li>
</ol>

docs.title: Line Break & Horizontal Rule
docs.text:
<br>   <!-- Line break -->
<hr>   <!-- Horizontal line -->

docs.title: Nesting and Indentation
docs.text:
Elements can be nested inside others. Always indent child elements to improve readability.

<ul>
  <li>
    <a href="#">Nested Link</a>
  </li>
</ul>

docs.title: Comments in HTML
docs.text:
<!-- This is a comment -->

Used to add notes or temporarily disable parts of code.

docs.title: 🔹<div> — Block-level Container
docs.text:
• Block-level element
  • Takes up the full width of its parent
  • Starts on a new line
  • Commonly used to group large sections of HTML

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
<div>
  <h2>Welcome</h2>
  <p>This is a section grouped using a div.</p>
</div>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Welcome

This is a section grouped using a div.

docs.title: Use Cases:
docs.text:
• Layout sections
  • Wrapping multiple elements
  • Styling or scripting group of elements

docs.title: 🔹 <span> — Inline Container
docs.text:
• Inline element
  • Takes only as much width as necessary
  • Does not break into a new line
  • Used to style or script inline text portions

docs.title: Example:
docs.text: <p>This is a <span style="color: red;">highlighted</span> word.</p>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: This is a highlighted word.

docs.title: Use Cases:
docs.text:
• Highlight part of a sentence
  • Apply CSS to a few words
  • Target with JavaScript for inline elements

docs.title: 🔹Paragraph
docs.text:
<p>This is a paragraph</p>
<br />
<p>This is a paragraph 2</p>
<hr />
  • <p>: Paragraph Tag
  • <br />: Break Rule
  • <hr />: Horizontal Rule

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
This is a paragraph



This is a paragraph 2

docs.title: 🔹Headings
docs.text:
<h1>Heading 1</h1>
<h2>Heading 2</h2>
<h3>Heading 3</h3>
<h4>Heading 4</h4>
<h5>Heading 5</h5>
<h6>Heading 6</h6>

docs.title: Output:Heading 3
docs.text:
Heading 1
Heading 2

Heading 4
Heading 5
Heading 6

docs.title: 1. Unordered List: <ul>
docs.text:
• Used for lists without a specific order
  • List items are marked with bullets by default
<ul>
  <li>HTML</li>
  <li>CSS</li>
  <li>JavaScript</li>
</ul>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
• HTML
  • CSS
  • JavaScript

docs.title: 2. Ordered List: <ol>
docs.text:
• Used for lists where the order matters
  • List items are marked with numbers by default
<ol>
  <li>Wake up</li>
  <li>Brush teeth</li>
  <li>Start coding</li>
</ol>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
1. Wake up
  2. Brush teeth
  3. Start coding

docs.title: Type Attribute:
docs.text:
You can use the type attribute to change numbering:

<ol type="A">
  <li>Step One</li>
  <li>Step Two</li>
</ol>

Types:

  • 1 – Default (numbers)
  • A – Uppercase letters
  • a – Lowercase letters
  • I – Uppercase Roman numerals
  • i – Lowercase Roman numerals

docs.title: 3. Description List: <dl>
docs.text: • Used for name/value pairs such as terms and definitions

docs.title: Syntax:
docs.text:
<dl>
  <dt>HTML</dt>
  <dd>HyperText Markup Language</dd>
  <dt>CSS</dt>
  <dd>Cascading Style Sheets</dd>
</dl>
  • <dl> — Description List
  • <dt> — Description Term
  • <dd> - Description Definition

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: HTMLHyperText Markup LanguageCSSCascading Style Sheets

docs.title: Nesting Lists
docs.text:
Lists can be nested inside one another. Used for:

  • Navigation menus
  • Step-by-step instructions
  • Glossaries
  • FAQs
  • Feature lists
<ol>
  <li>Frontend
    <ul>
      <li>HTML</li>
      <li>CSS</li>
    </ul>
  </li>
  <li>Backend
    <ul>
        <li>NodeJS</li>
        <li>ExpressJS</li>
        <li>MySQL</li>
    </ul>
  </li>
  and much more!
</ol>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
1. Frontend
    • HTML
    • CSS
    • JavaScript
  2. Backend
    • NodeJS
    • ExpressJS
    • MySQL
and much more!

docs.title: 1. Block-Level Elements
docs.text:
• Always start on a new line
  • Occupy the full width of the parent container
  • Can contain other block or inline elements
  • Commonly used for layout and structure

docs.title: Common Block Elements:
docs.text:
• <div>
  • <p>
  • <h1> to <h6>
  • <ul>, <ol>, <li>
  • <section>, <article>, <header>, <footer>
  • <table>, <form>, <nav>

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
<div>
  <h2>Heading</h2>
  <p>This is a paragraph inside a block-level div.</p>
</div>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Heading
This is a paragraph inside a block-level div.

docs.title: 2. Inline Elements
docs.text:
• Do not start on a new line
  • Occupy only as much width as necessary
  • Can only contain other inline elements or text
  • Commonly used for formatting small chunks of text

docs.title: Common Inline Elements:
docs.text:
• <span>
  • <a>
  • <strong>, <em>, <b>, <i>
  • <img>
  • <input>, <label>
  • <code>, <mark>, <abbr>

docs.title: Example:
docs.text: <p>This is a <span style="color: red;">highlighted</span> word.</p>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: This is a highlighted word.

docs.title: 3. Mixed Example
docs.text:
<div>
  <p>This is a <strong>bold</strong> word and a <a href="#">link</a>.</p>
</div>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: This is a bold word and a link.

docs.title: 4. Converting Display Type with CSS
docs.text:
You can convert block elements to inline or vice versa using CSS:

<style>
  div {
    display: inline;
  }

  span {
    display: block;
  }
</style>

docs.title: 1. Basic Form Structure
docs.text:
<form action="/submit" method="POST">
  <label for="name">Name:</label>
  <input type="text" id="name" name="username">
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Name:

Submit

docs.title: 2. Common Input Types
docs.text:
<input type="text">
<input type="password">
<input type="email">
<input type="number">
<input type="date">
<input type="file">
<input type="checkbox">
<input type="radio">
<input type="submit">
<input type="reset">

docs.title: 3. Label: <label>
docs.text:
• Associates text with a specific input
  • Clicking the label focuses the input field
<label for="email">Email:</label>
<input type="email" id="email" name="email">

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Email:

docs.title: 4. Select Dropdown: <select>
docs.text:
<label for="language">Choose a language:</label>
<select id="language" name="language">
  <option value="html">HTML</option>
  <option value="css">CSS</option>
  <option value="js">JavaScript</option>
</select>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Choose a language:

  HTML
  CSS
  JavaScript

docs.title: 5. Text Area: <textarea>
docs.text:
Used for multi-line input (e.g., comments or messages)

<label for="msg">Message:</label>
<textarea id="msg" name="message" rows="4" cols="30"></textarea>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Message:

docs.title: 6. Radio Buttons and Checkboxes
docs.text:
<label>
  <input type="radio" name="gender" value="male"> Male
</label>
<label>
  <input type="radio" name="gender" value="female"> Female
</label>

<label>
  <input type="checkbox" name="subscribe" checked> Subscribe to newsletter
</label>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Male
 Female

 Subscribe to newsletter

docs.title: 7. Fieldset and Legend
docs.text:
• Group related form elements together
<fieldset>
  <legend>Login Info</legend>
  <label>Username: <input type="text" name="user"></label><br>
  <label>Password: <input type="password" name="pass"></label>
</fieldset>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Login Info
  Username: 
  Password:

docs.title: 8. Button: <button>
docs.text:
<button type="submit">Submit Form</button>
<button type="reset">Reset Form</button>
<button type="button" onclick="alert('Clicked!')">Click Me</button>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Submit Form
Reset Form
Click Me

docs.title: 9. Form Attributes
docs.text: Attribute Description action URL where the form data will be sent method HTTP method to use (GET or POST) autocomplete Enables or disables autocomplete novalidate Disables default browser validation

docs.title: 10. Input Attributes
docs.text: Attribute Description type Specifies the input type name Name for form submission value Pre-filled value placeholder Hint text inside input required Makes input mandatory readonly Makes input non-editable disabled Disables the input min, max Minimum/maximum values (for number/date) pattern Regex pattern for validation

docs.title: 1. Image: <img>
docs.text:
• Embeds images into the webpage
  • Inline element
  • Requires src and alt attributes
<img src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/1563356/pexels-photo-1563356.jpeg" alt="Image" width="200" height="100">

docs.title: 2. Audio: <audio>
docs.text:
• Embeds audio files like .mp3, .ogg, etc.
  • Requires <source> inside
  • Needs controls for play/pause UI
<audio controls>
  <source src="music.mp3" type="audio/mpeg">
  Your browser does not support the audio element.
</audio>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Your browser does not support the audio element.

docs.title: 3. Video: <video>
docs.text:
• Embeds video content like .mp4, .webm
  • Use controls, autoplay, loop, muted, and poster attributes as needed
<video width="320" height="240" controls>
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  Your browser does not support the video tag.
</video>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Your browser does not support the video tag.

docs.title: 4. Multiple Sources: <source>
docs.text:
• Inside <audio> or <video>
  • Used to provide format fallback for cross-browser compatibility
<video controls>
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <source src="video.webm" type="video/webm">
  Your browser does not support the video tag.
</video>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Your browser does not support the video tag.

docs.title: 5. Subtitles and Captions: <track>
docs.text:
• Adds subtitles, captions, descriptions, or metadata to video
  • Placed inside <video>
<video controls>
  <source src="video.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  <track src="subtitles.vtt" kind="subtitles" srclang="en" label="English" default>
</video>

docs.title: 6. Canvas: <canvas>
docs.text:
• Used to draw graphics via JavaScript
  • Requires a JS script to render anything
<canvas id="myCanvas" width="200" height="100"></canvas>

<script>
  const canvas = document.getElementById('myCanvas');
  const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d');
  ctx.fillStyle = "red";
  ctx.fillRect(20, 20, 150, 50);
</script>

docs.title: 7. Iframe: <iframe>
docs.text:
• Embeds other web content like maps, videos, or sites
  • Use loading="lazy" for performance
<iframe src="https://example.com" width="600" height="300" loading="lazy"></iframe>

docs.title: 9. Lazy Loading Media
docs.text:
• Helps improve performance by deferring image or iframe loading
  • Supported in modern browsers
<img src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/1563356/pexels-photo-1563356.jpeg" loading="lazy" alt="Lazy Image">
<iframe src="https://example.com" loading="lazy"></iframe>

docs.title: Semantic Tags
docs.text:
Semantic tags clearly describe the meaning of the content inside them — making HTML more readable, accessible, and SEO-friendly.

They help both developers and browsers understand the structure and purpose of the content.

docs.title: 1. <header>
docs.text:
• Represents the header of a section or page
  • Typically contains logo, navigation, or page titles
<header>
  <h1>My Portfolio</h1>
  <nav>
    <a href="#projects">Projects</a>
    <a href="#contact">Contact</a>
  </nav>
</header>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
My PortfolioProjects
Contact

docs.title: 2. <nav>
docs.text:
• Defines navigation links
  • Typically used inside a <header>, <aside>, or separately
<nav>
  <a href="/">Home</a>
  <a href="/about">About</a>
  <a href="/services">Services</a>
</nav>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Home
About
Services

docs.title: 3. <main>
docs.text:
• Represents the main content of the page
  • Should only be used once per page
<main>
  <h2>Welcome to My Website</h2>
  <p>This is where the main content goes.</p>
</main>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Welcome to My Website
This is where the main content goes.

docs.title: 4. <section>
docs.text:
• Represents a standalone section of content
  • Useful for grouping related content blocks
<section>
  <h2>Projects</h2>
  <p>Here are some of my recent works.</p>
</section>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Projects
Here are some of my recent works.

docs.title: 5. <article>
docs.text:
• Represents independent, reusable content — like blog posts, news articles, or comments
<article>
  <h2>Blog Post Title</h2>
  <p>This is the content of the blog post.</p>
</article>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
Blog Post Title
This is the content of the blog post.

docs.title: 6. <aside>
docs.text:
• Represents side content — like sidebars, ads, or related links
  • Should be related to the main content
<aside>
  <h3>Related Posts</h3>
  <ul>
    <li><a href="#">Post 1</a></li>
    <li><a href="#">Post 2</a></li>
  </ul>
</aside>

docs.title: Output:Related Posts
docs.text:
• Post 1
  • Post 2

docs.title: 7. <footer>
docs.text:
• Represents the footer of a section or page
  • Commonly includes copyright, contact info, or navigation
<footer>
  <p>© 2025 Aman Verma. All rights reserved.</p>
</footer>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: © 2025 Aman Verma. All rights reserved.

docs.title: 8. <figure> and <figcaption>
docs.text:
• Used to wrap media (image, chart, video) and its caption
<figure>
  <img src="https://as1.ftcdn.net/v2/jpg/05/40/08/54/1000_F_540085480_WN26Tz5VOFRwdPsLmK73JXNuSYsi2luw.jpg" alt="Project Screenshot">
  <figcaption>Screenshot of my latest project</figcaption>
</figure>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Screenshot of my latest project

docs.title: 9. <mark> (Highlight Text)
docs.text:
• Highlights or emphasizes text
<p>I want to <mark>highlight</mark> this word.</p>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: I want to highlight this word.

docs.title: 1. HTML Entities
docs.text: HTML Entities are used to display reserved characters that would otherwise be interpreted as HTML.

docs.title: Common Entities:
docs.text: Character Entity Code Description < < Less than > > Greater than & & Ampersand " " Double quote ' ' Single quote   Non-breaking space

docs.title: Example:
docs.text: <p>5 < 10 && 10 > 5</p>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: 5 < 10 && 10 > 5

docs.title: 2. <code> — Inline Code
docs.text:
• Displays a short snippet of code
  • Renders using a monospaced font
  • Usually used inside paragraphs
<p>Use the <code><section></code> tag for grouping content.</p>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Use the <section> tag for grouping content.

docs.title: 3. <pre> — Preformatted Text
docs.text:
• Preserves whitespace, tabs, and line breaks
  • Often used to display blocks of code
<pre>
function greet(name) {
  console.log("Hello, " + name);
}
</pre>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text:
function greet(name) {
  console.log("Hello, " + name);
}

docs.title: 4. <kbd> — Keyboard Input
docs.text:
• Used to show what the user should type
  • Usually renders in a monospace font
<p>Press <kbd>Ctrl</kbd> + <kbd>C</kbd> to copy.</p>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Press Ctrl + C to copy.

docs.title: 5. <samp> — Sample Output
docs.text:
• Represents sample output from a program
<p>Output: <samp>Hello, World!</samp></p>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: Output: Hello, World!

docs.title: 6. <var> — Variable Name
docs.text:
• Used to represent a variable in programming or math
<p>The value of <var>x</var> is 10.</p>

docs.title: Output:
docs.text: The value of x is 10.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: JavaScript (JS) is a high-level, interpreted programming language used to make web pages interactive — handling user events, updating content dynamically, validating forms, and much more.

docs.title: a) Inline
docs.text: <button onclick="alert('Clicked!')">Click Me</button>

docs.title: b) Internal (inside <script> tag)
docs.text:
<script>
  console.log("Hello, world!");
</script>

docs.title: c) External (best practice)
docs.text: <script src="script.js"></script>

docs.title: 2. Output Methods
3. Variables
docs.text:
let name = "Aman";
const PI = 3.14;
var age = 15;
  • let – block-scoped (preferred)
  • const – cannot be reassigned
  • var – function-scoped (avoid using)

docs.title: 4. Data Types
docs.text:
• String — "Hello"
  • Number — 42, 3.14
  • Boolean — true, false
  • Null — null
  • Undefined — undefined
  • Object — { key: "value" }
  • Array — [1, 2, 3]
let x = "Hi";           // string
let y = 123;            // number
let isActive = true;    // boolean
let user = { name: "Aman", age: 16 }; // object

docs.title: 5. Comments
docs.text:
// Single-line comment

/*
  Multi-line
  comment
*/

docs.title: 6. Basic Operators
docs.text:
let a = 5 + 3;     // 8
let b = 10 - 4;    // 6
let c = 2 * 3;     // 6
let d = 8 / 2;     // 4
let e = 9 % 4;     // 1
let f = 2 ** 3;    // 8 (exponent)

docs.title: 7. Basic DOM Manipulation
docs.text:
<p id="demo">Text</p>
<script>
  document.getElementById("demo").innerText = "Changed!";
</script>

JavaScript can select, modify, and respond to HTML elements.

docs.title: 8. Why JavaScript?
docs.text:
• Runs in the browser (no install needed)
  • Works with HTML & CSS for full interactivity
  • Essential for modern web apps (forms, animation, logic, APIs)

docs.title: Variables
docs.text: Variables are used to store data that can be referenced and manipulated later.

docs.title: a) var
docs.text:
• Function-scoped
  • Hoisted (can be used before declaration, but unsafe)
  • Avoid using in modern code
var x = 10;

docs.title: b) let
docs.text:
• Block-scoped
  • Can be reassigned
  • Preferred for values that change
let count = 5;
count = 6;

docs.title: c) const
docs.text:
• Block-scoped
  • Cannot be reassigned
  • Must be initialized when declared
  • Use for constants or fixed references
const PI = 3.14;
// PI = 3.1415 ❌ Error

docs.title: 2. Naming Rules
docs.text:
• Case-sensitive
  • Must start with letter, _, or $
  • Can’t start with a number
  • Use camelCase for naming (userName, totalPrice)
let userName = "Emma";
const MAX_SCORE = 100;

docs.title: 3. Hoisting Behavior
docs.text:
• var is hoisted (declared at top of scope), but value is undefined
  • let and const are hoisted but not initialized (Temporal Dead Zone)
console.log(x); // undefined
var x = 10;

console.log(y); // ReferenceError
let y = 20;

docs.title: 4. Reassignment
docs.text: Keyword Reassignable Scope var ✅ Function let ✅ Block const ❌ Block

docs.title: 5. Constants with Objects and Arrays
docs.text:
const prevents reassignment of the variable, not mutation of its contents.

const user = { name: "Rahul" };
user.name = "Dev"; // ✅ Allowed

const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // ✅ Allowed

docs.title: Data Types
docs.text: JavaScript has 8 basic data types, divided into primitive and reference (non-primitive) types.

docs.title: 1. Primitive Types (Immutable)
docs.text: Stored directly in the variable.

docs.title: a) String
docs.text:
Text data.

let name = "Henry";

docs.title: b) Number
docs.text:
Both integers and floats.

let age = 15;
let pi = 3.14;

docs.title: c) Boolean
docs.text:
Logical values.

let isOnline = true;

docs.title: d) undefined
docs.text:
A variable declared but not assigned.

let x;
console.log(x); // undefined

docs.title: e) null
docs.text:
Intentional absence of value.

let data = null;

docs.title: f) Symbol (ES6)
docs.text:
Unique and immutable identifier.

let sym = Symbol("id");

docs.title: g) BigInt (ES2020)
docs.text:
Handles large integers beyond Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER.

let big = 1234567890123456789012345678901234567890n;

docs.title: 2. Reference Types (Objects)
docs.text: Stored as references in memory.

docs.title: a) Object
docs.text:
Collection of key-value pairs.

let user = {
  name: "Aman",
  age: 15
};

docs.title: b) Array
docs.text:
Indexed list of values.

let scores = [90, 85, 100];

docs.title: c) Function
docs.text:
Functions are objects with callable behavior.

function greet() {
  console.log("Hi");
}

docs.title: 3. Type Checking
docs.text:
Use typeof to check types:

typeof "hello";     // "string"
typeof 42;          // "number"
typeof null;        // "object" (bug in JS)
typeof [];          // "object"
typeof undefined;   // "undefined"

To check arrays:

Array.isArray([1, 2, 3]); // true

docs.title: 4. Dynamic Typing
docs.text:
JavaScript is dynamically typed, so variables can change type at runtime.

let x = "hello";
x = 123; // valid

docs.title: Objects
docs.text: Objects are key-value pairs used to store structured data. Each key is a property, and its value can be any data type.

docs.title: a) Object Literal
docs.text:
const user = {
  name: "John",
  age: 30,
  isAdmin: true
};

docs.title: b) Using new Object()
docs.text:
const user = new Object();
user.name = "John";
user.age = 30;

docs.title: a) Dot Notation
docs.text: console.log(user.name); // "John"

docs.title: b) Bracket Notation
docs.text:
console.log(user["age"]); // 30

Use brackets when:

  • The key has spaces or special characters
  • Accessing dynamically
let key = "isAdmin";
console.log(user[key]); // true

docs.title: 3. Modifying Properties
docs.text:
user.name = "Mike";
user["age"] = 25;

docs.title: 4. Adding Properties
docs.text: user.email = "test@example.com";

docs.title: 5. Deleting Properties
docs.text: delete user.isAdmin;

docs.title: 6. Nested Objects
docs.text:
const product = {
  name: "Phone",
  specs: {
    ram: "8GB",
    storage: "128GB"
  }
};

console.log(product.specs.ram); // "8GB"

docs.title: 7. Methods (Functions inside objects)
docs.text:
const person = {
  firstName: "Jane",
  greet() {
    console.log("Hello!");
  }
};

person.greet(); // "Hello!"

docs.title: for...in loop
docs.text:
for (let key in user) {
  console.log(key, user[key]);
}

docs.title: 9. Built-in Object Methods
docs.text:
Object.keys(user);    // ["name", "age"]
Object.values(user);  // ["John", 30]
Object.entries(user); // [["name", "John"], ["age", 30]]

docs.title: Conditionals
docs.text: Conditionals control the flow of code by executing different blocks based on true/false evaluations.

docs.title: 1. if Statement
docs.text:
let age = 18;

if (age >= 18) {
  console.log("Adult");
}

docs.title: 2. if...else
docs.text:
if (age >= 18) {
  console.log("Adult");
} else {
  console.log("Minor");
}

docs.title: 3. if...else if...else
docs.text:
if (age < 13) {
  console.log("Child");
} else if (age < 18) {
  console.log("Teen");
} else {
  console.log("Adult");
}

docs.title: 4. Ternary Operator
docs.text:
Shorthand for if...else.

let msg = (age >= 18) ? "Adult" : "Minor";
console.log(msg);

docs.title: 5. Logical Operators
docs.text: Operator Name Example Result && AND a > 0 && b > 0 true if both ` ` OR `a > 0 b > 0` true if either ! NOT !isLoggedIn inverse

docs.title: 6. switch Statement
docs.text:
Efficient alternative to multiple if...else if.

let color = "blue";

switch (color) {
  case "red":
    console.log("Red selected");
    break;
  case "blue":
    console.log("Blue selected");
    break;
  default:
    console.log("No match");
}
  • break is required to prevent fall-through
  • default runs if no case matches

docs.title: 7. Falsy Values
docs.text:
These values are considered false in conditionals:

  • false
  • 0
  • "" (empty string)
  • null
  • undefined
  • NaN
if (!value) {
  console.log("Falsy value");
}

docs.title: Loops
docs.text: Loops let you repeat a block of code multiple times, either a fixed number of times or until a condition fails.

docs.title: 1. for Loop
docs.text:
Use when the number of iterations is known.

for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
  console.log(i);
}
  • i = 0 → start
  • i < 5 → condition
  • i++ → increment

docs.title: 2. while Loop
docs.text:
Runs while the condition is true.

let i = 0;
while (i < 5) {
  console.log(i);
  i++;
}

docs.title: 3. do...while Loop
docs.text:
Runs at least once, then checks the condition.

let i = 0;
do {
  console.log(i);
  i++;
} while (i < 5);

docs.title: 4. for...of Loop
docs.text:
Iterates over iterables like arrays, strings, etc.

let nums = [10, 20, 30];

for (let num of nums) {
  console.log(num);
}

docs.title: 5. for...in Loop
docs.text:
Iterates over keys in an object.

const user = { name: "John", age: 30 };

for (let key in user) {
  console.log(key, user[key]);
}

docs.title: break: exits the loop early.
docs.text:
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
  if (i === 5) break;
  console.log(i); // 0 to 4
}

docs.title: continue: skips current iteration.
docs.text:
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
  if (i === 2) continue;
  console.log(i); // 0, 1, 3, 4
}

docs.title: 7. Looping Arrays with .forEach()
docs.text:
let arr = [1, 2, 3];

arr.forEach(function (val) {
  console.log(val);
});

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Functions are reusable blocks of code that perform specific tasks. You define them once and call them anywhere.

docs.title: 1. Function Declaration
docs.text:
function greet() {
  console.log("Hello");
}

greet(); // Call the function

docs.title: 2. Parameters & Arguments
docs.text:
function add(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

let result = add(5, 3); // 8
  • a, b → parameters (placeholders)
  • 5, 3 → arguments (actual values)

docs.title: 3. Return Value
docs.text:
Use return to send a value back.

function multiply(x, y) {
  return x * y;
}

If no return, the function returns undefined.

docs.title: 4. Function Expression
docs.text:
Function stored in a variable.

const greet = function () {
  console.log("Hi");
};

greet();

docs.title: 5. Arrow Functions (ES6)
docs.text:
Shorter syntax, often used for callbacks.

const add = (a, b) => a + b;

With multiple lines:

const greet = () => {
  console.log("Hello");
};

docs.title: 6. Default Parameters
docs.text:
function sayHello(name = "Guest") {
  console.log("Hello, " + name);
}

sayHello(); // "Hello, Guest"

docs.title: 7. Rest Parameters
docs.text:
Combines multiple arguments into an array.

function sum(...nums) {
  return nums.reduce((a, b) => a + b);
}

sum(1, 2, 3); // 6

## 8. Anonymous Functions

Functions without a name, often used inline.

```js
setTimeout(function () {
  console.log("Delayed");
}, 1000);

docs.title: 9. Callback Functions
docs.text:
Function passed as an argument to another function.

function process(data, callback) {
  callback(data);
}

process(5, function (num) {
  console.log(num * 2);
});

docs.title: Strings
docs.text: Strings represent text and are one of the most commonly used data types in JavaScript.

docs.title: 1. Creating Strings
docs.text:
let str1 = "Hello";
let str2 = 'World';
let str3 = `Hello, ${str2}`; // Template literal

docs.title: 2. String Length
docs.text:
let name = "John";
console.log(name.length); // 4

docs.title: 3. Accessing Characters
docs.text:
let str = "JavaScript";
console.log(str[0]);      // "J"
console.log(str.charAt(2)); // "v"

docs.title: toUpperCase() / toLowerCase()
docs.text:
"abc".toUpperCase(); // "ABC"
"XYZ".toLowerCase(); // "xyz"

docs.title: indexOf() / lastIndexOf()
docs.text:
"hello".indexOf("l");      // 2
"hello".lastIndexOf("l");  // 3

docs.title: includes()
docs.text: "hello".includes("he"); // true

docs.title: startsWith() / endsWith()
docs.text:
"hello".startsWith("he"); // true
"hello".endsWith("lo");   // true

docs.title: slice(start, end)
docs.text: "JavaScript".slice(0, 4); // "Java"

docs.title: substring(start, end)
docs.text: "JavaScript".substring(4, 10); // "Script"

docs.title: substr(start, length) (deprecated)
docs.text: "JavaScript".substr(4, 3); // "Scr"

docs.title: replace() / replaceAll()
docs.text:
"apple pie".replace("pie", "juice"); // "apple juice"
"aaa".replaceAll("a", "b"); // "bbb"

docs.title: trim(), trimStart(), trimEnd()
docs.text: "  hello  ".trim(); // "hello"

docs.title: split()
docs.text: "red,green,blue".split(","); // ["red", "green", "blue"]

docs.title: repeat(n)
docs.text: "ha".repeat(3); // "hahaha"

docs.title: 5. Template Literals
docs.text:
Use backticks ` for multiline and interpolation.

let name = "Alice";
let msg = `Hello, ${name}!`;

docs.title: 6. Escape Characters
docs.text:
let str = "He said: \"Hello\"";
let path = "C:\\Users\\User";

docs.title: 7. Comparing Strings
docs.text:
"abc" === "abc"; // true
"abc" > "abd";   // false (lexical order)

docs.title: Arrays
docs.text: Arrays are ordered lists used to store multiple values in a single variable. They can contain any data type — strings, numbers, objects, functions, etc.

docs.title: 1. Creating Arrays
docs.text:
let arr = [10, 20, 30];
let empty = new Array(); // []

docs.title: 2. Accessing Items
docs.text:
arr[0];     // 10
arr.length; // 3
arr[arr.length - 1]; // Last item

docs.title: 3. Modifying Items
docs.text:
arr[1] = 99;       // [10, 99, 30]
arr.push(40);      // [10, 99, 30, 40]
arr.pop();         // Removes last → [10, 99, 30]
arr.shift();       // Removes first → [99, 30]
arr.unshift(5);    // Adds to front → [5, 99, 30]

docs.title: push() / pop()
docs.text:
arr.push(100); // add to end
arr.pop();     // remove from end

docs.title: shift() / unshift()
docs.text:
arr.shift();     // remove first
arr.unshift(0);  // add to start

docs.title: slice(start, end)
docs.text:
Returns a shallow copy of a portion.

let sliced = arr.slice(1, 3); // [99, 30]

docs.title: splice(start, deleteCount, ...items)
docs.text:
Modifies the array (insert/remove/replace).

arr.splice(1, 1);         // Remove 1 item at index 1
arr.splice(1, 0, 88);     // Insert 88 at index 1

docs.title: indexOf() / includes()
docs.text:
arr.indexOf(30);   // 2
arr.includes(99);  // true

docs.title: join()
docs.text: arr.join(", "); // "5, 99, 30"

docs.title: reverse()
docs.text: arr.reverse(); // In-place reverse

docs.title: sort()
docs.text:
arr.sort(); // Lexicographic sort
arr.sort((a, b) => a - b); // Numeric sort

docs.title: for loop
docs.text:
for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
  console.log(arr[i]);
}

docs.title: for...of
docs.text:
for (let val of arr) {
  console.log(val);
}

docs.title: forEach()
docs.text:
arr.forEach((val, i) => {
  console.log(i, val);
});

docs.title: map()
docs.text: let doubled = arr.map(n => n * 2);

docs.title: filter()
docs.text: let evens = arr.filter(n => n % 2 === 0);

docs.title: reduce()
docs.text: let sum = arr.reduce((acc, curr) => acc + curr, 0);

docs.title: 7. Nested Arrays
docs.text:
let grid = [[1, 2], [3, 4]];
console.log(grid[1][0]); // 3

docs.title: 1. What Is the DOM?
docs.text:
DOM (Document Object Model) is a tree-like structure where:

  • Each HTML element is a node
  • JavaScript can access and manipulate these nodes

The root is document.

docs.title: 2. Accessing the Document
docs.text:
console.log(document);           // entire DOM
console.log(document.title);     // page title
console.log(document.body);      // <body> element

docs.title: a) getElementById
docs.text:
Selects a single element by ID.

let heading = document.getElementById("main-title");

docs.title: b) getElementsByClassName
docs.text:
Returns a live HTMLCollection (not an array).

let items = document.getElementsByClassName("menu-item");

Access with index: items[0]

docs.title: c) getElementsByTagName
docs.text:
Returns all elements by tag name.

let allParas = document.getElementsByTagName("p");

docs.title: querySelector
docs.text:
Returns first match using CSS selector.

let btn = document.querySelector(".btn");       // first .btn
let header = document.querySelector("#header"); // #header
let input = document.querySelector("input[type='text']");

docs.title: querySelectorAll
docs.text:
Returns NodeList of all matches.

let allButtons = document.querySelectorAll(".btn");

Loop with forEach():

allButtons.forEach(btn => btn.classList.add("active"));

docs.title: 5. NodeList vs HTMLCollection
docs.text:
Feature NodeList HTMLCollection Returned by querySelectorAll getElementsBy* Can use forEach() ✅ (modern browsers) ❌ (convert to array) Live updates ❌ static ✅ live

To convert HTMLCollection:

Array.from(items).forEach(el => console.log(el));

docs.title: innerText
docs.text:
Returns visible text.

element.innerText = "Hello";

docs.title: textContent
docs.text:
Returns all text, including hidden.

element.textContent = "Hi";

docs.title: innerHTML
docs.text:
Reads or replaces the HTML inside an element.

element.innerHTML = "<strong>Bold text</strong>";

docs.title: 2. Changing Styles
docs.text:
Use .style.propertyName (camelCase for CSS).

element.style.color = "red";
element.style.backgroundColor = "#f0f0f0";

docs.title: add() – Add a class
docs.text: element.classList.add("active");

docs.title: remove() – Remove a class
docs.text: element.classList.remove("disabled");

docs.title: toggle() – Add if missing, remove if present
docs.text: element.classList.toggle("open");

docs.title: contains() – Check if class exists
docs.text: element.classList.contains("active"); // true/false

docs.title: getAttribute()
docs.text: let link = element.getAttribute("href");

docs.title: setAttribute()
docs.text: element.setAttribute("src", "img.png");

docs.title: removeAttribute()
docs.text: element.removeAttribute("disabled");

docs.title: 5. Input Values
docs.text:
Use .value for form elements.

let input = document.querySelector("#username");
console.log(input.value);       // get
input.value = "newUser123";     // set

docs.title: 6. Example: Modify Button Text & Style
docs.text:
let btn = document.querySelector("#submit");

btn.innerText = "Sending...";
btn.style.backgroundColor = "gray";
btn.classList.add("disabled");

docs.title: 1. createElement()
docs.text:
Creates a new DOM element (not in the document yet).

let newDiv = document.createElement("div");
newDiv.innerText = "Hello World";

docs.title: 2. append() and appendChild()
docs.text:
Adds element to the end of a parent.

document.body.append(newDiv);       // accepts text, multiple elements
parent.appendChild(newDiv);         // older, only one node

docs.title: prepend()
docs.text:
Adds to the start of a parent.

parent.prepend(newDiv);

docs.title: 3. insertBefore()
docs.text:
Inserts a node before another inside the same parent.

parent.insertBefore(newNode, referenceNode);

docs.title: 4. remove() (modern)
docs.text:
Removes an element directly.

element.remove();

docs.title: 5. removeChild() (older)
docs.text:
Used on the parent, passing the child to remove.

parent.removeChild(child);

docs.title: 6. replaceChild()
docs.text:
Replaces one child with another.

parent.replaceChild(newNode, oldNode);

docs.title: cloneNode(deep)
docs.text:
• true → clone entire element with children
  • false → only clone the node itself
let clone = original.cloneNode(true);
parent.append(clone);

docs.title: 8. Example: Add New List Item
docs.text:
let li = document.createElement("li");
li.innerText = "New Item";

document.querySelector("ul").appendChild(li);

docs.title: 1. What Are Events?
docs.text: Events are actions that happen in the browser (click, scroll, keypress, input, etc.). You can run functions in response.

docs.title: 2. addEventListener()
docs.text:
Attach a function to run when an event occurs.

element.addEventListener("click", function () {
  console.log("Clicked");
});

docs.title: Common events:
docs.text:
• click
  • mouseover / mouseout
  • keydown / keyup
  • input
  • submit
  • change

docs.title: 3. Named Function Example
docs.text:
function greet() {
  console.log("Hello");
}

button.addEventListener("click", greet);

docs.title: 4. Arrow Function Example
docs.text:
button.addEventListener("click", () => {
  console.log("Clicked!");
});

docs.title: 5. Accessing the Event Object
docs.text:
Every event listener gets an event object with info about the action.

element.addEventListener("click", function (event) {
  console.log(event.target); // the element that was clicked
});

docs.title: 6. Prevent Default Behavior
docs.text:
Use event.preventDefault() to stop default actions like form submit or link navigation.

form.addEventListener("submit", function (e) {
  e.preventDefault();
  console.log("Form blocked");
});

docs.title: 7. Event Delegation
docs.text:
Listen to a parent and handle clicks from child elements using event.target.

document.querySelector("ul").addEventListener("click", function (e) {
  if (e.target.tagName === "LI") {
    e.target.classList.toggle("selected");
  }
});

docs.title: 8. Removing Event Listeners
docs.text:
function handler() {
  console.log("Removed after one use");
  btn.removeEventListener("click", handler);
}

btn.addEventListener("click", handler);

docs.title: 9. once Option
docs.text:
Automatically removes listener after first trigger.

btn.addEventListener("click", () => {
  console.log("Clicked once");
}, { once: true });

docs.title: Callbacks
docs.text: A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function, to be called later — either after an operation completes or at a specific time.

docs.title: 1. Basic Callback Example
docs.text:
function greet(name) {
  console.log("Hello, " + name);
}

function processUser(callback) {
  let name = "Alice";
  callback(name);
}

processUser(greet); // "Hello, Alice"

docs.title: 2. Anonymous Callback
docs.text:
setTimeout(function () {
  console.log("Executed after 1 second");
}, 1000);

docs.title: 3. Arrow Function Callback
docs.text:
setTimeout(() => {
  console.log("Arrow callback");
}, 500);

docs.title: forEach
docs.text:
[1, 2, 3].forEach(num => {
  console.log(num);
});

docs.title: map
docs.text: let doubled = [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2);

docs.title: filter
docs.text: let even = [1, 2, 3, 4].filter(n => n % 2 === 0);

docs.title: 5. Why Use Callbacks?
docs.text:
• Handle asynchronous tasks (API calls, timeouts)
  • Reuse logic
  • Custom behavior passed into functions

docs.title: 6. Callback Hell (Bad Practice)
docs.text:
Nested callbacks make code hard to read:

doTask1(() => {
  doTask2(() => {
    doTask3(() => {
      console.log("Done");
    });
  });
});

Use Promises or async/await to fix this.

docs.title: Promises
docs.text: A Promise is an object representing the eventual result of an asynchronous operation — it may resolve (success) or reject (failure).

docs.title: 1. Creating a Promise
docs.text:
const promise = new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
  let success = true;

  if (success) {
    resolve("Done");
  } else {
    reject("Error");
  }
});

docs.title: 2. Consuming a Promise
docs.text:
promise
  .then(result => {
    console.log("Resolved:", result);
  })
  .catch(error => {
    console.log("Rejected:", error);
  });

docs.title: 3. Example: Simulated Async Task
docs.text:
function asyncTask() {
  return new Promise((resolve) => {
    setTimeout(() => {
      resolve("Task complete");
    }, 1000);
  });
}

asyncTask().then(res => console.log(res));
